segments.s252-g33: بفرمود is a past/honorific verb meaning 'he commanded/said' and introduces a reported order. زان پس is a temporal phrase ('after that') made of زان ('from that') + پس ('after/then') and sets the sequence. که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the subordinate clause; گیرند is a present/subjunctive-ish plural verb 'they take/seize' (here deontic/imperative reported as part of the order). راه is the direct object 'road/way' of گیرند.
segments.s252-g34: بدان is a prepositional combination meaning 'to that/into that' (here written as ب + دان); the segment functions as a destination phrase modifying راه. نامداران is an adjectival plural 'the famed/renowned ones'. جوینده is a participial noun 'seekers' describing those named. گاه here follows as a noun that denotes a locus/occasion tied to the seekers (classical Persian often uses گاه to form compounds meaning 'place/time of X'). The whole phrase supplies a destination/qualifier: 'to that place of the famed seekers.'
segments.s252-g35: ز هر سو is a circumstantial prepositional phrase 'from every side/quarter' (ز = 'from', هر سو = 'every side'). خروش تکاپوی is a nominal phrase in which خروش 'cry/uproar' is qualified by تکاپوی 'of struggle/bustle' (the ezāfe is omitted in verse), i.e. 'a cry and tumult.' خاست is the past/preterite verb 'arose/began' and predicates the whole phrase.
segments.s252-g36: ز خون ریختن is a verbal-nominal construction where ز خون ('from/because of blood') + ریختن (the infinitive/masdar 'the spilling') together mean 'from the spilling of blood.' بر درش is a locative/target phrase 'on/at his gate' (بر = 'on/against', در = 'gate/door', ش = enclitic 'his'). جوی خاست is a poetic image: جوی 'a stream' + خاست 'arose' = 'a stream sprang up' (i.e. blood ran like a stream).
segments.s252-g37: هرآنکس که آمد is a relative/indefinite clause 'whoever came' (هرآنکس = 'whoever', که = complementizer, آمد = 'came'). ز توران سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'from Turan, with an army/host' (ز = 'from/of', توران = 'Turan', سپاه = 'army/host') specifying the source/party of those who came.
segments.s252-g38: زمانه تهی ماند reads as a verbless/result clause where زمانه (here 'fortune/the world') is the subject and تهی ماند 'was left empty / became void' is the predicate (تهی = 'empty/devoid', ماند = 'remained/was left'). زو جایگاه is a short oblique phrase 'of him / his place' (ز ــ + ــو = 'from him' / 'his'), so the whole means 'fortune left his place empty' i.e. 'he was left without place/status.'
segments.s252-g39: گرفتند here is a past transitive verb 'they took' or 'they seized upon' and governs the following verbal noun phrase. بر کینه جستن شتاب is a compound phrase: بر کینه جستن = 'to seek/seek after revenge' (بر = upon/toward, کینه = 'hatred/revenge', جستن = 'to search/seek') and شتاب 'haste' indicates manner ('they took haste to seek revenge' = they hurried to revenge). The whole clause expresses a collective sudden resolve.
segments.s252-g40: ازان خانه is a locative phrase 'from that house' (ازان = 'from that', خانه = 'house'). بگریخت is a past/formal verb 'he fled/ran away' (the prefix ب‑ is the classical preverb), and افراسیاب is the proper name in the subject position; together: 'Afrasiab fled from that house.'
segments.s252-g65: منیژه is a proper name (subject). نشسته is a past-participial/stative predicate (‘seated’), forming a nominal clause with the name. بخیمه درون is a locative phrase: بخیمه = 'in the tent' (ب- prefix + خیمه) and درون reinforces 'inside'; word order is Name + predicate + locative.
segments.s252-g66: پرستنده is a noun (‘adorer, devoted follower’) functioning as an appositive/nominal continuation (no new finite verb). بر پیش او is a prepositional locative expression meaning 'before/in front of her' (بر + پیش + او). رهنمون is a predicate noun 'guide' describing the پرستنده; the whole phrase gives the admirer’s position/role relative to Manijeh.
segments.s252-g67: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain/one'. داستان زد is an idiom where زدن with داستان means 'to tell/recite a tale' (زد is simple past). تهمتن is the agent (the one who told it). بروی is an archaic/elliptical particle here attached after the verb; its exact force is idiomatic (often marking utterance or direction) and is syntactically subordinate to the verb phrase.
segments.s252-g68: که‌گر (= که گر) introduces a subordinate conditional 'that/if'. می‌بریزد is present/imperfective ('it pours / is shed') with the present prefix می-. نریزدش is a negative jussive/subjunctive form 'may it not fall on him/it' where the enclitic ش refers back to the thing whose scent is meant. بوی is the noun 'scent' (object of the clause).
segments.s252-g69: چنینست is a fixed copular phrase 'such is / it is thus'. رسم is the noun 'custom' or 'practice'. سرای سپنج is a genitive construction 'the house/of the sponge' (سرای = 'house/household' followed by سپنج in genitive); here سپنج (literally 'sponge') is metaphorical. Note: the supplied lemma senses for سرای do not show the noun 'house', so the gloss treats سرای as the household/place noun.
segments.s252-g70: گهی ... گهی is a correlative pair 'sometimes ... and sometimes ...'. ناز و نوش is a paired nominal phrase meaning 'naaz (affection/pampering) and noosh (delight)'. درد و رنج is the corresponding pair 'pain and suffering'. The repeated و simply coordinates the items.
segments.s252-g25: 'براندیش' is an imperative (think/consider) formed from براندیشیدن — a direct command to reflect. 'زان' is a demonstrative 'that/​from that' pointing to the throne; it precedes the noun phrase تخت فرخنده‌جای, where تخت (throne) is modified by the compound adjective فرخنده‌جای (auspicious/fortunate place). Word order is imperative + demonstrative + noun phrase (typical poetic inversion).
segments.s252-g26: This clause describes the result/state. 'مرا' is the accusative 'me' (object). 'بسته' is a past participle/adjective 'bound' (hands tied); it modifies or describes the state of 'me'. 'در پیش کرده' is a perfective construction with کرده (has made/placed) and the compound در پیش 'in front/ before (it)'; together 'has placed (me) before (it)'. 'بپای' (from پاییدن) here means 'to lie in wait / to be set as a watch' and expresses purpose/result. Note the object (مرا) appears before the participle, a common Persian order.
segments.s252-g27: 'همی' is an archaic/poetic imperfective marker here giving an ongoing or habitual sense ('ever/continually' or emphatic 'did so'); it modifies the verb 'جستی'. 'رزم جستی' literally 'you leapt/you rushed to battle' — جستی (past/preterite of جستن) is used in the sense 'to spring/​leap' here. 'بسان پلنگ' is a simile 'like a leopard' following the verb phrase.
segments.s252-g28: This is a contrasting clause about the speaker. 'مرا' (given as two pieces in the word list) is the object 'me'. 'دست‌بسته' is a compound adjective 'hands-bound' (bound-handed). 'بکردار' is a poetic/older form meaning 'in the manner/condition of' or 'in such a state' (here treated as an adjectival phrase introducing the comparison), and 'سنگ' ('stone/rock') completes the image: the speaker is motionless/stone-like while the addressee leapt. Note the compact, image-driven adjective sequence typical of epic diction.
segments.s252-g29: 'کنونم' is a temporal adverb 'now' with a final clitic-like m (poetic form). 'گشاده' is an adjective 'open' (here of attitude or access). 'بهامون' means 'to/for us' (به + امون, dialectal/poetic form) and is the beneficiary/recipient phrase. 'ببین' is the imperative 'see/observe' addressing the addressee; the verb invites the hearer to notice the speaker's openness.
segments.s252-g30: 'که' introduces the content/purpose clause ('so that' / 'that'). 'با من' literally 'with me' here functions as 'against me' or 'toward me' in the sense of targeting the speaker. 'نجوید' is the negative present form (ن + جوید) meaning 'does not / may not seek' (here 'may not seek'). 'ژیان' is 'life', 'شیر' 'lion', and 'کین' 'vengeance/hatred' — together 'شیر کین' is a compound epithet 'vengeful lion' or 'lion of vengeance'; the clause prays/declares that such a creature will not seek the speaker's life. Note: negation is carried by the n- prefix on the verb.
segments.s252-g31: 'بزد' is a simple past third-person 'he struck/struck (at)'. 'دست' is the object 'hand' and 'بر جامه افراسیاب' is a prepositional phrase 'upon Afrasiab's garment/robe' (بر + جامه + proper name). The verb + object + بر-phrase order is straightforward past narration.
segments.s252-g32: 'که' here introduces the consequential clause ('so that' / 'and so'), 'جنگ‌آوران را' is the definite object 'the warriors' (را marks the object). 'ببستست' is a past/heavy perfective verb meaning 'he/it closed' or 'fastened' — in context it means 'caused to cease' / 'put (them) into' — and 'خواب' is 'sleep'. Together the clause says the action produced sleep among the warriors: 'it put the warriors to sleep.'
segments.s252-g9: سران را is topicalized/definite (را) — 'as for the heads'; بسی is a quantifier meaning 'many/much' placed before the noun phrase it scopes over; سر جدا شد ز تن is a compressed predication: جدا شد (past, intransitive literal 'became separate') describes what happened to سر (head) and ز تن marks the source 'from the body'. The construction thus reads distributively: 'many a head became separated from the body.'
segments.s252-g10: پر از X is a fixed pattern 'full of X' (پر + از + noun). Here two parallel phrases are coordinated by و: پر از خاک ریش = 'beard full of dust' and پر از خون دهن = 'mouth full of blood.' The nouns ریش and دهن follow their modifiers (خاک, خون) in a poetic, slightly inverted order, and the possessive relation to the severed heads is understood from context.
segments.s252-g11: ز دهلیز در is a prepositional/locative phrase in poetic order: z (from) + دهلیز (vestibule/corridor) + در (here 'door/entrance'), so 'from the corridor at the door.' رستم is the subject and آواز داد is a light-verb idiom 'gave a voice' = 'shouted/called' in the past tense (داد = 'gave'). Word order is topical preposition phrase → subject → verbal phrase.
segments.s252-g12: که introduces the following wish/blessing clause. خواب تو خوش باد is an optative formula: adjective خوش followed by باد ('may it be') expresses 'may your sleep be pleasant.' The second clause is parallel: گردانت شاد = 'may your troop(s) be joyful'; گردانت carries the possessive suffix -ت ('your'). The conjunction و links the two parallel wishes.
segments.s252-g13: بخفتی تو بر گاه is past tense 'you slept' (بخفتی) with تو as the subject; بر گاه = 'on/at the royal seat' (گاه here used as place/occasion, poetic for 'palace/throne'). The coordination و then places بیژن بچاه in apposition/contrast: 'and Bijan (was) in the well' — the verb for Bijan is omitted (elliptical), a common poetic compression meaning 'Bijan lay in the well.'
segments.s252-g14: مگر introduces a rhetorical/excepting question 'unless/perchance'; باره here means 'a shower/burst' (of missiles), دیدی is past 'did you see?', ز آهن = 'of iron' (i.e., iron weapons/arrows), and براه is an adverbial 'along the road' or 'on the way.' The phrase literally asks 'did you not see a shower of iron on the road?'
segments.s252-g15: منم is emphatic 'I am' (with the copula built into the clitic); the following sequence is appositive/title information: رستم زابلی = 'Rostam of Zabul' and پور زال = 'son (pour) of Zal.' This is a self-identification in fixed-name/title order.
segments.s252-g16: نه negates the clause that follows: 'نه هنگام خوابست' — 'this is not the time for sleep' (هنگام = 'time/occasion', خوابست functions as 'for sleeping' / 'sleep-time' in this phrase). The coordination 'و آرام و هال' adds 'nor calm/peace nor repose/moment' — a quick list of things inappropriate now. The verb/copula is distributed across the coordinated nouns (elliptical style).
segments.s252-g1: برفتند is a past tense, 3rd-person plural verb (they went/they departed). با + proper name Rustom indicates accompaniment (‘with Rostam’). آن is a demonstrative ('that/those') modifying هفت گرد; گرد here is a noun whose precise historical meaning (a group or type of warrior) is not supplied in the provided senses, so it is left unspecified.
segments.s252-g2: This clause has the recipient (بنه اشکش) placed before the direct object (تیزهش) which is marked by the object marker را; سپرد is a 3rd-person singular past tense verb meaning 'he entrusted/handed over.' تیزهش is a possessive form (تیزه + -ش) 'its/his tip,' and را marks it as the definite object. بنه اشکش functions here as the indirect object/recipient (a name or title not fully glossed by the given senses).
segments.s252-g3: عنانها is the plural definite form of عنان ('the reins') and functions as the object. فگندند is a past, 3rd-person plural verb 'they threw/cast.' The prepositional phrase بر پیش زین ('on/upon the front/forepart of the saddle') specifies the direction/place of the throwing: بر = 'on/upon', پیش = 'in front/ahead', زین = 'saddle'.
segments.s252-g4: کشیدند is past, 3rd-person plural 'they drew' (here meaning 'drew weapons'). یکسر is an adverbial intensifier meaning 'at once' / 'entirely'; همه 'all' modifies تیغ 'swords'; تیغ کین is a genitive/construct where کین ('vengeance') qualifies the swords as 'swords of vengeance.' Word order is verb → adverb → subject/objects.
segments.s252-g5: بشد here is an impersonal/past verbal form meaning 'it came to pass / they arrived' (literary use). تا is used in the sense 'as far as / up to' introducing the destination (بدرگاه = 'in the presence/royal court of'), and افراسیاب is the proper name 'Afrasiab.' Together they mean 'they came to Afrasiab's court.'
segments.s252-g6: بهنگام is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'at the time of' or 'during.' The three nouns joined by و ('and') — سستی (laxity/weakness), آرام (calm/quiet), خواب (sleep) — specify the circumstances/time when they reached the court: i.e., at a moment of slackness, calm, and sleep in the court.
segments.s252-g7: برآمد is a past/preterite verb 'arose/issued forth.' ز (poetic form of از) means 'from,' and ناگه is an adverb 'suddenly/unexpectedly.' The nouns joined by و (ده و دار و گیر) are three places/locations ('village' = ده, 'abode/house' = دار, and a third place or stronghold گیر) from which the action issues; they form a coordinated source phrase.
segments.s252-g8: درخشیدن is the infinitive/verbal noun 'the gleaming' or 'to gleam' used nominally; here it names the phenomenon that rose. تیغ (sword) is the agent/thing that gleams. و باران تیر is a coordinated noun phrase: باران ('a shower') qualified by تیر ('of arrows') to mean 'a shower/rain of arrows.' The whole phrase functions as the content that burst forth from the places named above.
segments.s252-g41: بکاخ اندر آمد is a frozen/prepositional fronting meaning "entered the palace" (بـ + کاخ = 'to/into the palace', اندر = 'in/into', آمد = 'came/entered'). خداوند رخش is a nominal phrase 'the lord/master of Rakhsh' (Rakhsh is the horse's name) and serves as the subject: literally 'into the palace came the master of Rakhsh'.
segments.s252-g42: همه فرش و دیبای او is the object phrase 'all his carpets and brocades' (همه = 'all', فرش = 'carpet', دیبای = 'brocade', او = possessive 'his' in context). کرد بخش is a light-verb construction in which کرد ('made/did') combines with the noun بخش ('gift') to mean 'made (them) a gift' or 'gave them away.' Word order is typical classical Persian: object phrase before the verbal phrase.
segments.s252-g43: پریچهرگان سپهبدپرست is a compound nominal phrase: پریچهرگان ('fair‑faced beauties') modified by سپهبدپرست ('worshipping/adoring the spahbed/commander'). The second element functions adjectivally/epithetically, identifying those beauties as devotees of the commander.
segments.s252-g44: گرفته is a perfect/past participle/verb 'have seized' agreeing with the preceding subject (the beauties). همه modifies the following noun phrase (here 'everyone/all'), دست‌گردان is an epithet meaning 'fickle' or 'roving‑handed' describing those taken, and بدست is a locative/agentive phrase 'by the hand' or 'in hand' — together: 'they took everyone by the hand, the fickle‑handed.'
segments.s252-g45: گرانمایه اسبان و زین پلنگ is a coordinated noun phrase: گرانمایه ('precious/costly') modifies اسبان ('horses'), and زین پلنگ ('leopard/panther saddle') is joined by و ('and'). The phrase lists splendid mounts and their ornate trappings.
segments.s252-g46: نشانده is a past participle 'placed/set'; its object is گهر ('a gem'). در جناغ خدنگ is a locative phrase 'in the breast (جناغ) of the dagger (خدنگ)', a poetic way to describe the dagger's central hilt/plate. Together: 'they set a jewel in the dagger's breast.'
segments.s252-g47: ازان پس ('after that') is a temporal phrase introducing the sequence. ز ایوان ببستند بار literally 'from the iwan they bound/tied the load' (ز = 'from', ایوان = 'iwan/portico', ببستند = 'they tied up/bound', بار = 'load/baggage'). The verb ببستند carries the sense of packing/taking down baggage.
segments.s252-g48: بتوران نکردند is a negative perfect/preterite 'they did not go to Turan' (بتوران = 'to Turan', نکردند = 'they did not do/perform' used here with the sense of not setting out). بس روزگار is an adverbial phrase meaning 'for many a day' / 'for a long time' (بس intensifies the duration; روزگار = 'time/days').
segments.s252-g57: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing reported speech; من بیگمانم is a first‑person present stative ('I am certain') with the proclitic -م marking 'I'. کزین پس (an archaic compound of ک+از+این + پس) = 'from this after' = 'henceforth'. بکین is a poetic third‑person plural present/future (the final -ند is omitted in poetry) meaning 'they take/they will take (vengeance)'—here sense 'take vengeance'.
segments.s252-g58: سیه گردد is a predicative clause 'become black' (گردد = become; classical/historical present used for narration). از + NP ('از سم اسبان زمین') is an ablative/causal phrase 'from/by the hooves of the horses on the ground' with اسبان in the genitive of سم and زمین indicating the surface; the whole means 'the earth will be blackened by the horses' hoofs.'
segments.s252-g59: This phrase places the verb سازد ('makes/raises') before the subject (افراسیاب) — a common poetic inversion. گشن لشکری = 'a hungry army' (گشن = 'hungry' modifying لشکری). سازد here is a present/historical present meaning 'he raised/assembled' (Afrasiab is the agent at the end).
segments.s252-g60: بنیزه is an instrumental/adverbial 'with spears'; بپوشد is the verb 'he covered/veiled' (ب‑ prefix + پوشد) used here as a narrative/historical present 'he covered'. رخ آفتاب = 'the face of the sun' (the ezāfe linking is omitted as is common in poetry). The clause means 'he covered the sun's face with spears' (a vivid martial image).
segments.s252-g61: برفتند is simple past 'they went/advanced'. یکسر here is an adverb 'one and all/entirely' modifying the action; سواران جنگ = 'the horsemen (for) battle' (object/topic 'جنگ' is understood as the destination or purpose). Word order is verb → adverb → subject → object, a frequent poetic arrangement.
segments.s252-g62: همه رزم را is 'all/the whole battle' with the object marker را; تیز کردند is past 'they made sharp' (کردند = 'they did/made' with adjectival complement تیز = 'sharp'), and چنگ ('claw/talon') is used metaphorically as the thing sharpened — i.e. 'they all sharpened their talons/arms for battle.' The clause uses poetic inversion and metaphor to describe preparation for combat.
segments.s252-g63: همه نیزه‌داران = 'all the spear-bearers' (subject). زدوده is a past participle from زدودن meaning 'cleansed/polished' and modifies سنان ('spears'/'spearheads'), so زدوده سنان = 'with cleansed/polished spearheads' (i.e. bright/ready spears). The past-participle modifier follows the noun cluster in the poetic word order.
segments.s252-g64: همه جنگ را = 'the whole battle' with the object marker را; گرد کرده is a compound past participle/verb ('having gathered/turned together' from گرد کردن) and عنان = 'reins'. Together 'گرد کرده عنان' = 'gathered the reins' (i.e. tightened control/prepared to charge). The clause uses را to mark 'جنگ' as the domain/purpose for which they gather the reins.
segments.s252-g17: شکستم is a 1st‑person past tense (perfect) verb with the suffix -am: literally “I broke / I was broken,” here best rendered as “I am broken / I am crushed” with a present/result sense. The locative phrase در بند زندان تو follows and names the state/place (در = “in”), with بند (fetter, bond) and زندان (prison) placed before the possessor تو (you) in classical poetic order.
segments.s252-g18: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause (“for/that”), explaining why the speaker is broken. سنگ گران is noun + adjective (stone + heavy). بد نگهبان تو is a noun phrase meaning “a bad/poor guardian of you,” where بد modifies نگهبان and the possessor تو follows without the ezāfe (typical in older poetic diction).
segments.s252-g19: رها شد is a two‑word verbal sequence where رها (free) plus شد (past of شدن) yields the intransitive past “was freed / became free.” The phrase سر و پای بیژن names the affected parts (“head and feet of Bijan”) and ز بند is an ablative prepositional phrase (“from the bond(s)/prison”) giving source.
segments.s252-g20: بداماد (‘to/for the bridegroom’ or simply ‘the groom’) is a preposed noun phrase serving as the agent/topic; بر کس نسازد گزند is a negative present/subjunctive construction where ن‑ on نسازد makes it negative (‘will not cause/make’). Here نسازد uses the basic sense “to make/cause,” so the whole clause means “the groom will not cause harm to anyone.”
segments.s252-g21: ترا is the older/poetic object/benefactive form of تو (t + را fused) meaning “for you” or “to you.” The nouns رزم و کین (‘battle and vengeance’) are given, followed by the name سیاوخش which modifies/possesses those nouns by juxtaposition (classical genitive without a linking particle). بس at the end is a predicative adverb meaning “enough” or “suffices.”
segments.s252-g22: بدین here functions as a locative/demonstrative adverbial (“in/with this” / “in this [plain]”), followed by دشت (“plain”). گردیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive ‘to roam/turn/wander’ that takes رخش (Rakhsh, the steed) as its logical subject; the clause states that Rakhsh’s roaming the plain is sufficient, with بس again closing the clause (“is enough”).
segments.s252-g23: همیدون is a locative/name placed before the verb (classical poetic order) and sets the scene (“on/at Hamidun / on the same plain”). برآورد is a past tense verb ‘he brought forth/raised’ (third person past), بیژن is the subject (Bijan), and خروش is the object ‘a cry/uproar’—so ‘Bijan raised a cry’ with locative first.
segments.s252-g24: که‌ای here is an exclamatory/vocative sequence (classical poetic address) introducing the direct address to ترک (‘O Turk’). بدگوهر is a compound adjective meaning ‘ill‑natured / of vile character’ placed after ترک, and تیره هوش is another epithet ‘dark‑minded / of gloomy wit’ where تیره modifies هوش (mind/awareness). The whole is a vocative insult: “O Turk, ill‑natured, dark‑minded!”
segments.s252-g49: ز بهر is a prepositional phrase 'for the sake of / because of' (ز + بهر). Poetic word order places the verb تاخت ('galloped/rushed') before its subject اسبان ('horses'); بزور is an adverbial phrase 'by force' modifying the verb.
segments.s252-g50: بدان here is a compound of ب- + an older demonstrative/particle (roughly 'to/at that'); the exact lexical entry for دان in the list doesn't match this function, so that part is marked uncertain. تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that' and governs the following verb. نخیزد is negative present-subjunctive/optative 'may not arise / should not begin' (ن- + خیزد), and ازان کار شور means 'a tumult/commotion from that affair' with ازان 'from that'.
segments.s252-g51: چنان 'so' + رنجه (verbal noun/verb 'suffered') qualifies the manner: 'suffered so (much)'. بد is an adverbial/qualifying 'badly/ill' here modifying رنجه. از رنج راه is an ablative phrase 'from the hardship(s) of the road' giving the cause.
segments.s252-g52: که introduces a result clause 'so that / such that'. بر سرش 'on his head' (بر + سر + possessive ش). بر درد بود is an idiomatic structure 'there was (a) pain' (بر + درد + بود), i.e. 'pain was upon (him)'; از کلاه gives the source 'from the helmet'.
segments.s252-g53: This is a compact coordinate description: سواران ز بس رنج 'the riders, from so much suffering' and اسبان ز تگ 'and the horses from (tег)'. ز + noun gives the cause ('because of/from'). The odd word تگ is poetically terse; here it gives a short cause parallel to 'رنج' for the horses.
segments.s252-g54: این یک جمله خبری با تاکید است: یکی را را is the topical/object marker construction ('not even one'), بتن 'on the body' (ب + تن), بر نجنبید رگ is a negative past/perfect statement 'not a vein stirred' — نجنبید is the past/negative form of جنبیدن 'to move/stir', and رگ is the locus 'a vein'. Word order is emphatic (object/topic before verb).
segments.s252-g55: بلشکر is an adverbial/prepositional compound 'with an army / in force' modifying فرستاد ('sent'); the sequence is Subject (رستم) + verb (فرستاد) + object (پیام) in a straightforward past-tense narration.
segments.s252-g56: که introduces reported content 'that ...'. شمشیر کین 'the sword of vengeance' is the object; بر کشید is the verbal phrase 'drew up / pulled out' (بر + کشید) — a common idiom for drawing a sword from its sheath. کشید in the provided sense list lacks the usual 'to draw/pull' entry, so its listed entries don't match and are marked accordingly. از نیام specifies source 'from the scabbard'.

sentences.s252-s17: Then he ordered that the famed seekers take that road.
sentences.s252-s18: From every side there arose cries and a bustle; from the spilling of blood at his gate a stream sprang up.
sentences.s252-s19: Whoever came with the Turanian host was left by fortune without a place.
sentences.s252-s20: They began to hasten in seeking vengeance; Afrasiab fled from that house.
sentences.s252-s33: Manijeh, seated inside the tent, with a devoted admirer standing before her as a guide.
sentences.s252-s34: Tahmatan told a tale, saying, 'If it pours, may its scent not fall on him.'
sentences.s252-s35: Such is the custom of the house (literally: 'sponge-house'): sometimes caresses and delights, and sometimes pain and suffering.
sentences.s252-s13: Consider that auspicious throne; it has bound me and set me before it to lie in wait.
sentences.s252-s14: You leapt into battle like a leopard; I, with my hands bound, am like a stone.
sentences.s252-s15: Now behold how open I am toward you; may no vengeful lion seek my life.
sentences.s252-s16: He struck his hand on Afrasiab's robe, so that it put the warriors to sleep.
sentences.s252-s5: Many heads were severed from bodies; their beards were full of dust and their mouths full of blood.
sentences.s252-s6: From the corridor at the door Rostam cried out: 'May your sleep be pleasant and may your troops be glad.'
sentences.s252-s7: You slept on the throne while Bijan was in the well — unless, perchance, you saw a shower of iron along the road?
sentences.s252-s8: I am Rostam of Zabul, son of Zal; this is no time for sleep, nor for calm or repose.
sentences.s252-s1: Those seven gerd went with Rostam; he handed over his spear-tip to Baneh-Ashk.
sentences.s252-s2: They cast the reins forward onto the saddle; all at once they drew their swords of vengeance.
sentences.s252-s3: They reached the presence of Afrasiab at a time of weakness, calm, and sleep.
sentences.s252-s4: Suddenly from the village, dwelling, and stronghold there rose the gleam of swords and a shower of arrows.
sentences.s252-s21: The master of Rakhsh entered the palace and made all his carpets and brocades a gift.
sentences.s252-s22: The fair‑faced women, devoted to the spahbed, seized everyone by the hand — the fickle‑handed.
sentences.s252-s23: They arrayed costly horses and panther‑saddles, and set a jewel in the dagger's breast.
sentences.s252-s24: After that, from the iwan they packed up their load; they did not go to Turan for many days.
sentences.s252-s29: I am sure that from now on they will take vengeance, and the earth will be blackened by the horses' hooves.
sentences.s252-s30: Afrasiab raised a hungry army; with spears he covered the face of the sun.
sentences.s252-s31: The horsemen set out all at once for battle; they all sharpened for the fight, sharpening their talons.
sentences.s252-s32: All the spearmen, with polished spearheads, all gathered the reins for the battle.
sentences.s252-s9: I am broken in the fetters of your prison, for the heavy stone is a poor guard for you.
sentences.s252-s10: Bijan's head and feet were freed from the bonds; the groom will do no one harm.
sentences.s252-s11: For you, Siawakhsh's battle and vengeance suffice; Rakhsh roaming this plain is enough.
sentences.s252-s12: On that plain Bijan raised a cry: “O Turk of evil nature and dark wit!”
sentences.s252-s25: For the sake of (Baneh) the horses rushed forward with force so that from that affair no tumult would arise.
sentences.s252-s26: Rostam suffered so badly from the hardships of the road that there was pain upon his head from the helmet.
sentences.s252-s27: The horsemen, from so much hardship, and the horses, from (strain/narrowness), — not a single vein on one's body moved.
sentences.s252-s28: Rostam sent a message with (his) army, that he drew the sword of vengeance from its scabbard.
